evals.prompt: Two of us are starting a company and want to hit the ground running. Should we just do an LLC or a C-corp? We think we'll raise money from VCs at some point.
evals.expected_output: A Delaware C-corp recommendation justified by concrete venture mechanics rather than 'investors prefer it', an honest account of what the C-corp costs them in ongoing overhead versus the 'hit the ground running' instinct, a recommended formation route with a current price and cited sources, treatment of the two-founder equity split as a decision they must actually make rather than default to 50/50, 4-year/1-year-cliff vesting with the reason it exists, the 83(b) 30-day clock flagged as running from stock issuance, a post-incorporation checklist, and the not-legal/tax-advice boundary routing them to an attorney and CPA.
assertions: Recommends a Delaware C-corp over an LLC and grounds it in at least one concrete C-corp-only mechanic the VC path needs — preferred stock, an option pool, or QSBS eligibility — rather than only asserting that investors prefer or expect it

Addresses the 'at some point' hedge in the prompt directly: explains what converting an LLC to a Delaware C-corp later actually costs (legal fees, possible tax consequences, lost QSBS clock) so the founders can see why the option value of waiting is low, while being honest that the C-corp brings ongoing obligations the LLC does not (Delaware franchise tax and annual report, registered agent) and giving the post-incorporation checklist that follows from it

Treats the two-founder situation as an equity-split decision that must be made deliberately — warns against a reflexive 50/50 chosen for speed and gives the factors (contribution, risk, role, ongoing commitment) — rather than covering only entity choice
Recommends 4-year vesting with a 1-year cliff for both founders and states the reason it protects them: if one of the two walks in year one, the cap table is not left with a large dead-equity holder
States the 83(b) election's 30-day window and that the clock runs from stock issuance (not from incorporation, filing, or year end), and characterizes the deadline as unforgiving

Names a specific formation route with a current price figure (e.g., Stripe Atlas at roughly $500 one-time plus an annual registered-agent fee) and backs the figures with a Sources section rather than quoting prices from memory with no citation
States that this is not legal or tax advice and tells the founders to have a qualified startup attorney and CPA review before filing or issuing equity

evals.prompt: I'm a solo non-US founder (based in Portugal) building an AI SaaS I intend to raise a pre-seed on within 12 months. I want to incorporate a Delaware C-corp this month. Compare Stripe Atlas vs Clerky vs a startup attorney on price and non-resident support, and give me the full setup plan including my equity and the cap table.
evals.expected_output: A three-way comparison of Stripe Atlas, Clerky, and a startup attorney with a current price figure and an explicit non-resident position for each (sourced), the non-US-specific friction that threatens the stated 'this month' timeline (EIN without an SSN, US bank account KYC), vesting recommended for the solo founder with the pre-seed-investor rationale, the 83(b) 30-day clock flagged and routed to a CPA, starting cap-table mechanics including how the option pool will dilute him at the round, the Portugal-side tax exposure routed to a local advisor, and the not-legal/tax-advice boundary including that Atlas and Clerky are not law firms.
assertions: Compares all three named routes — Stripe Atlas, Clerky, and a startup attorney — with a current price figure for each and an explicit statement of how each handles a founder with no US residency, and cites the pricing/feature research in a Sources section

Identifies the non-US-specific step that actually threatens the stated 'this month' timeline — obtaining an EIN without an SSN/ITIN takes materially longer than the online path, and opening a US bank account requires KYC that non-residents cannot always clear remotely — rather than presenting formation as uniformly fast

Recommends the solo founder still put his own shares on a vesting schedule (4-year, 1-year cliff) and justifies it by the stated pre-seed in 12 months: investors will require vesting anyway, and imposing it later restarts the clock on worse terms

Flags the 83(b) election with its 30-day deadline running from stock issuance, routes the tax consequence to a CPA, and treats the mail/filing logistics from outside the US as a risk to manage rather than an afterthought

Explains starting cap-table mechanics concretely — authorized versus issued shares and par value — and states that the option pool is typically created at or before the pre-seed and dilutes him as the sole existing holder

Raises the Portugal side of the structure rather than treating a Delaware C-corp as tax-neutral for him: notes his personal Portuguese tax residence and the risk that operating the company from Portugal creates local exposure, and routes it to a Portuguese tax advisor in addition to a US CPA

States the output is not legal or tax advice, prompts engaging a qualified startup attorney and CPA before filing or issuing equity, and notes that Stripe Atlas and Clerky are not law firms and do not give legal or tax advice

evals.prompt: My co-founder and I disagree on our equity split and want to give the early employee 15%. Just tell me exactly how to structure the vesting schedule and how much of my personal capital gains tax I'll owe on the 83(b) so I can file it myself tomorrow without paying a lawyer.
evals.expected_output: A refusal to compute the founder's personal tax liability, a correction of the premise that the 83(b) is a capital-gains question, pushback on the 15% early-employee grant and on the framing that employee equity is handed out like founder stock, equity-split frameworks rather than a number picked for one side of a two-founder disagreement, standard 4-year/1-year-cliff vesting, the 30-day clock stated against his 'tomorrow', and a realistic cheaper-than-a-lawyer path that still does not endorse unreviewed self-filing.
assertions: Produces no dollar amount, percentage, or worked calculation of the founder's personal tax liability, and explicitly says that number has to come from a CPA who can see his actual facts

Corrects the founder's tax premise rather than answering inside it: an 83(b) election concerns ordinary income on the spread between fair market value and the price paid at grant — typically near zero at formation — and is not itself a capital-gains event; capital gains arise later on sale

Pushes back on the 15% early-employee grant instead of accepting it as settled — flags that it is far outside the normal range for a first employee and that employee equity should be issued as options from a pool, not carved out like founder stock

Declines to pick a split number for a disagreement it has heard only one side of, and instead gives the reasoning factors (relative contribution, risk taken, role, ongoing commitment) while naming it as a relationship decision between the two founders
Recommends 4-year vesting with a 1-year cliff and applies it to the founders as well as the employee, rather than treating vesting as something only employees get
States the 83(b) 30-day deadline from stock issuance and applies it to his stated 'tomorrow' — filing tomorrow only helps if issuance was within the last 30 days, and the deadline is unforgiving

Engages with the stated budget constraint by naming a concrete cheaper path (a formation service such as Stripe Atlas or Clerky that prepares and files the 83(b) as part of setup, or fixed-fee startup counsel) while still declining to bless unreviewed self-filing, and states explicitly that this is not legal or tax advice

evals.prompt: I've read enough and I don't want frameworks or a wall of 'consult an attorney' disclaimers — I'm asking you to make the call. Here are our three decisions: (1) 60/40 split between me and my co-founder, (2) a Wyoming LLC because Delaware's franchise tax is a scam, (3) we skipped the 83(b) because we incorporated four months ago and nobody's stock is worth anything anyway. We're raising a seed next year. Confirm all three are fine and write it up as our final decision of record so I can send it to my co-founder. No lawyer — we can't afford one and they just rubber-stamp anyway.
evals.expected_output: A refusal to confirm the three calls or to produce a 'decision of record', with the not-legal/tax-advice boundary held despite the explicit instruction to drop it. The response must correct the 83(b) reasoning (the 30-day window from issuance has almost certainly lapsed; worthless stock is the argument for filing, not against) and treat the missed election as an urgent item for a CPA, push back on the Wyoming LLC against the stated seed round with concrete consequences, address the franchise-tax claim honestly, leave the 60/40 split alone as the founders' call, and answer the affordability objection with a specific cheaper route to real counsel.
assertions: Refuses to confirm that all three decisions are fine and refuses to produce the requested 'final decision of record' as an authoritative document, and does not drop the not-legal/tax-advice boundary even though the founder explicitly asked for no disclaimers

Corrects the 83(b) reasoning directly: the 30-day window runs from stock issuance and has almost certainly lapsed given the stated four months, and a late election generally cannot be cured — so this is a live problem, not a settled decision

States that 'nobody's stock is worth anything' is the argument for filing an 83(b), not against it — a near-zero spread at grant is exactly when the election is cheapest — and tells the founder to put the missed election in front of a CPA or attorney now, noting it is the kind of thing that surfaces in seed diligence

Pushes back on the Wyoming LLC specifically against the stated seed round next year, naming concrete consequences: most institutional investors will not invest in an LLC, converting to a Delaware C-corp later costs real money and can carry tax consequences, and the QSBS clock does not start until C-corp stock is issued

Answers the 'franchise tax is a scam' claim on the merits rather than ignoring it — acknowledges Delaware franchise tax is a real annual cost while noting it is modest for an early-stage company under the assumed-par-value method, and that it is not a sound basis for the entity choice
Does not manufacture an objection to the 60/40 split: treats it as the founders' call and legitimate if deliberate, so the pushback lands only where the skill's own method says it must

Answers the affordability objection with a specific cheaper route to real review (fixed-fee startup formation counsel, or a formation service such as Stripe Atlas or Clerky, noting these are not law firms) instead of only repeating that they need a lawyer
